n140011: tn Heb “and Solomon son of David strengthened himself over his kingdom.”
n140012: tn The disjunctive clause (note the vav [‏ו‎] + subject pattern) probably has a causal nuance here.
n140016: sn The tabernacle was located in Gibeon; see 1 Chr 21:29.
n1400113: tn The cohortative with prefixed vav (‏ו‎) following the imperative here indicates purpose/result.
n1400114: tn Heb “so I may go out before this nation and come in.” The expression “go out…and come in” here means “to lead” (see HALOT 425 s.v. ‏יצא‎ qal.4).
n1400115: tn Heb “for.” The word “otherwise” is used to reflect the logical sense of the statement.
n1400116: tn Heb “who is able?” The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, “no one.”
n1400120: tn Heb “the life of those who hate you.”
n1400122: tn Heb “wisdom and discernment are given to you.”
n1400123: tn Heb “which was not so for the kings who were before you, and after you there will not be so.”
n1400124: tn Heb “and Solomon came from the high place which was in Gibeon [to] Jerusalem, from before the tent of meeting, and he reigned over Israel.”
n1400126: tn Heb “he placed them in the chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem.”
n1400127: tn The words “as plentiful” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1400129: sn The foothills (‏שְׁפֵלָה‎, shephelah) are the region between the Judean hill country and the Mediterranean coastal plain.
n1400130: sn Because Que is also mentioned, some prefer to see v. 16-17 as a reference to Mutsur. Que and Mutsur were located in Cilicia or Cappadocia (in modern southern Turkey). See HALOT 625 s.v. ‏מִצְרַיִם‎.
n1400131: tn Heb “and they brought up and brought out from Egypt a chariot for 600 silver (pieces), and a horse for 150, and in the same way to all the kings of the Hittites and to the kings of Aram by their hand they brought out.”
n140021: sn Beginning with 2:1, the verse numbers through 2:18 in the English Bible differ from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 2:1 ET = 1:18 HT, 2:2 ET = 2:1 HT, 2:3 ET = 2:2 HT, etc., through 2:18 ET = 2:17 HT. Beginning with 3:1 the verse numbers in the ET and HT are again the same.
n140022: tn Heb “and Solomon said to build a house for the name of the Lord and house for his kingship.”
n140026:
tc The parallel text of MT in 1 Kgs 5:16 has “thirty-three hundred,” but some Greek mss there read “thirty-six hundred” in agreement with 2 Chr 2:2, 18.
tn Heb “and 3,600 supervisors over them.”
n140027: tn Heb “Huram.” Some medieval Hebrew mss, along with the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate spell the name “Hiram,” agreeing with 1 Chr 14:1. “Huram” is a variant spelling referring to the same individual.
n140029: tn The words “help me” are supplied in the translation for clarification and stylistic reasons.
n1400210: tn Heb “cedars.” The word “logs” has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1400211: tn Heb “to build for him a house to live in it.”
n1400215: tn Or “heavens” (also in v. 12). The Hebrew term ‏שָׁמַיִם‎ (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
n1400216: tn Heb “Who retains strength to build for him a house, for the heavens and the heavens of heavens do not contain him? And who am I that I should build for him a house, except to sacrifice before him?”
n1400218: tn This is probably a variant name for almug trees; see 9:10-11 and the parallel passage in 1 Kgs 10:11-12; cf. NLT. One or the other probably arose through metathesis of letters.
n1400220: sn As a unit of dry measure a cor was roughly equivalent to six bushels (about 220 liters).
n1400221: tn Heb “20,000 baths” (also a second time later in this verse). A bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons (about 22 liters), so this was a quantity of about 120,000 gallons (440,000 liters).
n1400222: tn Heb “Huram” (also in v. 12). Some medieval Hebrew mss, along with the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate spell the name “Hiram,” agreeing with 1 Chr 14:1. “Huram” is a variant spelling referring to the same individual.
n1400223: tn Heb “who has given to King David a wise son knowing discernment and insight, who will build a house for the Lord and house for his kingship.”
n1400224: sn The name Huram Abi means “Huram [is] my father.”
n1400225: tn Heb “a son of a woman from the daughters of Dan, and his father a man of Tyre.”
n1400226: tn Heb “and we will cut down trees from Lebanon according to all your need.”
n1400227: tn Heb “to you,” but this phrase has not been translated for stylistic reasons—it is somewhat redundant.
n1400228: tn Or “on rafts.” See the note at 1 Kgs 5:9.
n1400230: sn The term ‏גֵּר‎ (ger) refers to a foreign resident, but with different social implications in different settings. In Mosaic Law the resident foreigner was essentially a naturalized citizen and convert to worshiping the God of Israel (see Exod 12:19, 48; Deut 29:10-13).
n1400234: tn Heb “and thirty-six hundred [as] supervisors to compel the people to work.”
n140032: tn Heb “where he.” “Lord” has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n140033: tn In 2 Sam 24:16 this individual is called ‏אֲרַוְנָא‎ (ʾaravna; traditionally “Araunah”). The form of the name found here also occurs in 1 Chr 21:15; 18-28.
n140034: sn This would be April-May, 966 b.c. by modern reckoning.
n140035: tn Heb “and these are the founding of Solomon to build the house of God.”
n140036: tn Heb “the length [in] cubits by the former measure was 60 cubits, and a width of 20 cubits.” Assuming a length of 18 inches (45 cm) for the standard cubit, the length of the foundation would be 90 feet (27 m) and its width 30 feet (9 m).
n140037: tc Heb “and the porch which was in front of the length corresponding to the width of the house, 20 cubits.” The phrase ‏הֵיכַל הַבַּיִת‎ (hekhal habbayit, “the main hall of the temple,” which appears in the parallel account in 1 Kgs 6:3) has been accidentally omitted by homoioarcton after ‏עַל־פְּנֵי‎ (’al pene, “in front of”). Note that the following form, ‏הָאֹרֶךְ‎ (haʿorekh, “the length”), also begins with the Hebrew letter he (‏ה‎). A scribe’s eye probably jumped from the initial he on ‏הֵיכַל‎ to the initial he on ‏הָאֹרֶךְ‎, leaving out the intervening letters in the process.
n140038: tc The Hebrew text has “one hundred and 20 cubits,” i.e., (assuming a cubit of 18 inches) 180 feet (54 m). An ancient Greek witness and the Syriac version read “20 cubits,” i.e., 30 feet (9 m). It is likely that ‏מֵאָה‎ (meʾah, “a hundred”) should be emended to ‏אַמּוֹת‎ (ʾammot, “cubits”).
n1400312: tn Heb “and he put up on it palm trees and chains.”
n1400313:
tn Heb “and he plated the house [with] precious stone for beauty, and the gold was the gold of Parvaim.”
sn The location of Parvaim, the source of the gold for Solomon’s temple, is uncertain. Some have identified it with modern Farwa in Yemen; others relate it to the Sanskrit parvam and understand it to be a general term for the regions east of Israel.
n1400314: tn Heb “the house of the holy place of holy places.”
n1400315: tn Heb “20 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), this would give a length of 30 feet (9 m).
n1400316: tc Heb “20 cubits.” Some suggest adding, “and its height 20 cubits” (see 1 Kgs 6:20). The phrase could have been omitted by homoioteleuton.
n1400317: tn The Hebrew word ‏כִּכַּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or, by extension, to a standard unit of weight. According to the older (Babylonian) standard the “talent” weighed 130 lbs. (58.9 kg), but later this was lowered to 108.3 lbs. (49.1 kg). More recent research suggests the “light” standard talent was 67.3 lbs. (30.6 kg). Using this as the standard for calculation, the weight of the gold plating was 40,380 lbs. (18,360 kg).
n1400318: tn Heb “and the wings of the cherubim, their length was 20 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the wingspan of the cherubim would have been 30 feet (9 m).
n1400319: tn Heb “the wing of the one was 5 cubits from the touching of the wall of the house, and the other wing was 5 cubits from the touching of the wing of the other cherub.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), each wing would have been 7.5 feet (2.25 m) long.
n1400320: tn Heb “and the wing of the one (‏הָאֶחָד‎, haʾekhad, “the one”; this should probably be emended to ‏הָאַחֵר‎, haʾakher, “the other”) cherub was 5 cubits, touching the wall of the house, and the other wing was 5 cubits, clinging to the wing of the other cherub.”
n1400321: tn Heb “the wings of these cherubim were spreading 20 cubits.”
n1400322: tn Heb “and they were standing on their feet, with their faces to the house.” An alternative translation of the last clause would be, “with their faces to the main hall.”
n1400323: sn The figure given here appears to refer to the combined length of both pillars (perhaps when laid end-to-end on the ground prior to being set up; cf. v. 17); the figure given for the height of the pillars in 1 Kgs 7:15, 2 Kgs 25:17, and Jer 52:21 is half this (i.e., 18 cubits).
n1400324: tc The Syriac reads “18 cubits” (27 feet). This apparently reflects an attempt at harmonization with 1 Kgs 7:15, 2 Kgs 25:17, and Jer 52:21.
n1400325: tn Heb “and he made before the house two pillars, 35 cubits [in] length, and the plated capital which was on its top [was] 5 cubits.” The significance of the measure “35 cubits” (52.5 feet or 15.75 m, assuming a cubit of 18 inches) for the “length” of the pillars is uncertain. According to 1 Kgs 7:15, each pillar was 18 cubits (27 feet or 8.1 m) high. Perhaps the measurement given here was taken with the pillars lying end-to-end on the ground before they were set up.
n1400326: tn The Hebrew text adds here, “in the inner sanctuary,” but the description at this point is of the pillars, not the inner sanctuary.
n1400327: tn Or “one on the south and the other on the north.”
n1400328: tn The name “Yakin” appears to be a verbal form and probably means, “he establishes.”
n1400329: tn The meaning of the name “Boaz” is uncertain. For various proposals, see BDB 126-27 s.v. ‏בֹּעַז‎. One attractive option is to revocalize the name as ‏בְּעֹז‎ (beʿoz, “in strength”) and to understand it as completing the verbal form on the first pillar. Taking the words together and reading from right to left, one can translate the sentence, “he establishes [it] in strength.”
n140041: tn Heb “20 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the length would have been 30 feet (9 m).
n140043: tn Heb “10 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the height would have been 15 feet (4.5 m).
n140044:
tn Heb “He made the sea, cast.”
sn The large bronze basin known as “The Sea” was mounted on twelve bronze bulls and contained water for the priests to bathe themselves (see v. 6; cf. Exod 30:17-21).
n140045: tn Heb “10 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the diameter would have been 15 feet (4.5 m).
n140046: tn Heb “5 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the height would have been 7.5 feet (2.25 m).
n140047: tn Heb “and a measuring line went around it 30 cubits all around.”
n1400410: tn Heb “all their hindquarters were toward the inside.”
n1400411: tn Heb “3,000 baths” (note that the capacity is given in 1 Kings 7:26 as “2,000 baths”). A bath was a liquid measure roughly equivalent to six gallons (about 22 liters), so 3,000 baths was a quantity of about 18,000 gallons (66,000 liters).
n1400413: tn Heb “Huram,” but here this refers to Huram Abi (2 Chr 2:13). The complete name has been used in the translation to avoid possible confusion with King Huram of Tyre.
n1400414: tn Heb “Huram finished doing all the work which he did for King Solomon [on] the house of God.”
n1400415: tn The words “he made” are added for stylistic reasons.
n1400416: tc The Hebrew text has ‏עָשָׂה‎ (ʿasah, “he made”), which probably should be emended to ‏עֶשֶׂר‎ (ʿeser, “ten”; see 1 Kgs 7:43).
n1400417: tc The Hebrew text has ‏עָשָׂה‎ (ʿasah, “he made”), which probably should be emended to ‏עֲשָׂרָה‎ (ʿasarah, “ten”; see 1 Kgs 7:43).
n1400418: tc Some prefer to read here “bowls,” see v. 11 and 1 Kgs 7:45.
n1400419: tn Heb “Huram Abi made for King Solomon [for] the house of the Lord.”
n1400421: tn Heb “Solomon made all these items in great abundance so that the weight of the bronze was not sought.”
n1400422:
tn Heb “the bread of the face/presence.”
sn This bread offered to God was viewed as a perpetual offering to God. See Lev 24:5-9.
n140052: sn The phrase the City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.
n140053: tn Heb “Then Solomon convened the elders of Israel, the heads of the tribes, the chiefs of the fathers belonging to the sons of Israel to Jerusalem to bring up the ark of the covenant of the Lord from the City of David (it is Zion).”
n140054: sn This festival in the seventh month was the Feast of Tabernacles, see Lev 23:34.
n140055: sn The seventh month would be September-October in modern reckoning.
n140057: tn Heb “and they carried the ark of the Lord…. The priests and the Levites carried them.”
n140058: tn Heb “And King Solomon and all the assembly of Israel, those who had been gathered to him, [were] before the ark, sacrificing sheep and cattle which could not be counted or numbered because of the abundance.”
n140059: tn The word “assigned” is supplied in the translation for clarification.
n1400510: sn These poles were used to carry the ark. The Levites were to carry it with the poles on their shoulders. See Exod 25:13-15; 1 Chr 15:15.
n1400512: sn Horeb is another name for Mount Sinai (cf. Exod 3:1).
n1400514: tn Heb “and when the priests went from the holy place.” The syntactical relationship of this temporal clause to the following context is unclear. Perhaps the thought is completed in v. 14 after a lengthy digression.
n1400515: tn Heb “Indeed [or “for”] all the priests who were found consecrated themselves without guarding divisions.”
n1400516: tn Heb “like one were the trumpeters and the musicians, causing one voice to be heard, praising and giving thanks to the Lord, and while raising a voice with trumpets and with cymbals and with instruments of music, and while praising the Lord.”
n1400517: tn Heb “and the house was filled with a cloud, the house of the Lord.”
n1400518: tn Heb “were not able to stand to serve.”
n140061: tn The words “O Lord” do not appear in the Hebrew text, but they are supplied in the translation for clarification; Solomon addresses the Lord in prayer at this point.
n140063: tn Heb “and he blessed all the assembly of Israel, and all the assembly of Israel was standing.”
n140064: tn The Hebrew text reads, “fulfilled by his hand,” but the phrase “by his hand” is somewhat redundant in contemporary English and has not been translated.
n140065: tn The Hebrew text reads, “promised by his mouth,” but the phrase “by his mouth” is somewhat redundant in contemporary English and has not been translated.
n140067: tn Heb “to build a house for my name to be there.” Here “name” is used by metonymy for the Lord himself, and thus the expression “to be there” refers to his taking up residence there (hence the translation, “a temple in which to live”). In this case the temple is referred to as a “house” where the Lord himself can reside.
n140068: tn Heb “for my name to be there.” See also the note on the word “live” in v. 5.
n140069:
tn Heb “and it was with the heart of David my father to build a house for the name of the Lord God of Israel.”
sn On the significance of the Lord’s “name,” see the note on the word “live” in v. 5.
n1400610: tn Heb “Because it was with your heart to build a house for my name, you did well that it was with your heart.”
n1400611: tn Heb “your son, the one who came out of your body, he will build the temple for my name.”
n1400612: tn Heb “5 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the length would have been 7.5 feet (2.25 m).
n1400614: tn Heb “3 cubits.” Assuming a cubit of 18 inches (45 cm), the height would have been 4.5 feet (1.35 m).
n1400616: tn Heb “one who keeps the covenant and the loyal love.”
n1400617: tn Heb “who walk before you with all their heart.”
n1400618: tn Heb “[you] who kept to your servant David my father that which you spoke to him.”
n1400619: tn Heb “you spoke by your mouth and by your hand you fulfilled, as this day.”
n1400620: tn Heb “there will not be cut off from you a man from before me sitting on the throne of Israel.”
n1400621: tn Heb “guard their way by walking in my law as you have walked before me.”
n1400623: tn Heb “Indeed, can God really live with mankind on the earth?” The rhetorical question expects the answer, “Of course not,” the force of which is reflected in the translation “God does not really live with mankind on the earth.”
n1400628: tn Heb “so your eyes might be open toward this house night and day, toward the place about which you promised to place your name there.”
n1400629: tn Heb “by listening to the prayer which your servant is praying concerning this place.”
n1400630: tn Heb “listen to the requests of your servant and your people Israel which they are praying concerning this place.”
n1400632: tn Heb “and if the man who sins against his neighbor when one takes up against him a curse to curse him and the curse comes before your altar in this house.”
n1400633: tn Heb “and you, hear [from] heaven and act and judge your servants by repaying the guilty, to give his way on his head, and to declare the innocent to be innocent, to give to him according to his innocence.”
n1400634: tn Or “are struck down before an enemy.”
n1400636: tn Heb “and they pray and ask for help.”
n1400638: tn Heb “when.” In the Hebrew text vv. 26-27a actually contain one lengthy conditional sentence, which the translation has divided into two sentences for stylistic reasons.
n1400639: tn Or “heavens” (also in v. 12). The Hebrew term ‏שָׁמַיִם‎ (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
n1400642: tn The Hebrew text reads “because you answer them,” as if the verb is from ‏עָנָה‎ (ʿanah, “answer”). However, this reference to a divine answer is premature, since the next verse asks for God to intervene in mercy. It is better to revocalize the consonantal text as ‏תְעַנֵּם‎ (teʿannem, “you afflict them”), a Piel verb form from the homonym ‏עָנָה‎ (“afflict”).
n1400643: tn The present translation understands ‏כִּי‎ (ki) in an emphatic or asseverative sense (“Certainly”). Other translations have “indeed” (NASB), “when” (NRSV), “so” (NEB), or leave the word untranslated (NIV).
n1400644: tn Heb “the good way in which they should walk.”
n1400646: tn Actually two Hebrew words appear here, both of which are usually (but not always) taken as referring to locusts. Perhaps different stages of growth or different varieties are in view, but this is uncertain. NEB has “locusts new-sloughed or fully grown”; NASB has “locust or grasshopper”; NIV has “locusts or grasshoppers”; NRSV has “locust, or caterpillar.”
n1400648: tn Heb “every prayer, every request for help which will be to all the people, to all your people Israel.”
n1400649: tn Heb “which they know, each his pain and his affliction.”
n1400650: tn The words “their sin” are not in the Hebrew text, but are supplied for clarification.
n1400651: tn Heb “and give to each one according to all his ways because you know his heart.” In the Hebrew text vv. 28-30a actually contain one lengthy conditional sentence, which the translation has divided up for stylistic reasons.
n1400652: tn Heb “Indeed you know, you alone, the heart of the sons of mankind.”
n1400656: tn Heb “your great name.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor (thus the translation here, “your great reputation”).
n1400657: tn Heb “and your strong hand and your outstretched arm.”
n1400658: tn Heb “and do all which the foreigner calls to [i.e., “requests of”] you.”
n1400659: tn Heb “name.” See the note on “reputation” in v. 32.
n1400661: tn Heb “that your name is called over this house which I built.” The Hebrew idiom “call the name over” indicates ownership. See 2 Sam 12:28.
n1400662: tn Heb “When your people go out for battle against their enemies in the way which you send them.”
n1400663: tn Heb “toward this city which you have chosen and the house which I built for your name.”
n1400664: tn Heb “their prayer and their request for help.”
n1400666: tn Heb “they”; the referent (God’s people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1400667: tn Heb “they”; the referent (God’s people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1400668: tn Or “stop and reflect”; Heb “bring back to their heart.”
n1400671: tn Heb “your name.” The word “name” sometimes refers to one’s reputation or honor (thus the translation here, “your honor”).
n1400672: tn Heb “their prayer and their requests for help.”
n1400674: tn Heb “May your eyes be open and your ears attentive to the prayer of this place.”
n1400676: tn Heb “and may your loyal ones rejoice in good.”
n1400677: tc Heb “do not turn away the face of your anointed ones.” Many medieval Hebrew mss, as well as the ancient versions, read the singular, “your anointed,” which would probably refer to Solomon specifically, rather than the people.
n140071: tn Or “the sky.” The Hebrew term ‏שָׁמַיִם‎ (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
n140072: tn The word “saying” is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n140073: tn Heb “and the priests were standing at their posts, and the Levites with the instruments of music of the Lord.”
n140074: tn Heb “which David the king made to give thanks to the Lord, for lasting is his loyal love, when David praised by them.”
n140075: tn Heb “opposite them”; the referent (the Levites) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n140076: tc The Hebrew text omits reference to the grain offerings at this point, but note that they are included both in the list in the second half of the verse (see note on “offerings” at the end of this verse) and in the parallel account in 1 Kgs 8:64. The construction ‏וְאֶת־הַמִּנְחָה‎ (veʾet-hamminkhah; vav [‏ו‎] + accusative sign + noun with article; “grain offerings”) was probably omitted accidentally by homoioarcton. Note the ‏וְאֶת‎ (veʾet) that immediately follows.
n140077: tn Heb “to hold the burnt sacrifice, the grain offering, and the fat portions.” Because this is redundant, the translation employs a summary phrase: “all these offerings.”
n140078: tn Heb “Solomon held the festival at that time for seven days, and all Israel was with him, a very great assembly from Lebo Hamath to the wadi of Egypt.”
n140079: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Solomon) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1400710: tn The words “they left” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n1400712: tn Heb “and all that entered the heart of Solomon to do in the house of the Lord and in his house he successfully accomplished.”
n1400714: tn Heb “temple of sacrifice.” This means the Lord designated the temple as the place for making sacrifices, and this has been clarified in the translation.
n1400716: tn Or “heavens.” The Hebrew term ‏שָׁמַיִם‎ (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
n1400717: tn Heb “the land,” which stands here by metonymy for the vegetation growing in it.
n1400718: tn Heb “over whom my name is called.” The Hebrew idiom “call the name over” indicates ownership. See 2 Sam 12:28.
n1400719: tn Heb “seek my face,” where “my face” is figurative for God’s presence and acceptance.
n1400722: sn Here the phrase heal their land means restore the damage done by the drought, locusts and plague mentioned in v. 13.
n1400723: tn Heb “my eyes will be open and my ears attentive to the prayer of this place.” Note Solomon’s request in 6:40.
n1400724: tn Heb “for my name to be there perpetually [or perhaps, “forever”].”
n1400725: tn Heb “and my eyes and my heart will be there all the days.”
n1400726:
tn Heb “As for you, if you walk before me, as David your father walked, by doing all which I commanded you, [and] you keep my rules and my regulations.”
sn Verse 17 is actually a lengthy protasis (“if” section) of a conditional sentence, the apodosis (“then” section) of which appears in v. 18.
n1400727: tn Heb “I will establish the throne of your kingdom.”
n1400728: tn Heb “there will not be cut off from you a man ruling over Israel.”
n1400729: tn The Hebrew pronoun is plural, suggesting that Solomon and all Israel (or perhaps Solomon and his successors) are in view. To convey this to the English reader, the translation “you people” has been employed.
n1400731: tn Heb “and walk and serve other gods and bow down to them.”
n1400732: tn Heb “them.” The switch from the second to the third person pronoun is rhetorically effective, for it mirrors God’s rejection of his people—he has stopped addressing them as “you” and begun addressing them as “them.” However, the switch is awkward and confusing in English, so the translation maintains the direct address style.
n1400733: tn Heb “them.” See the note on “you” earlier in this verse.
n1400734:
tc Instead of “I will throw away,” the parallel text in 1 Kgs 9:7 has “I will send away.” The two verbs sound very similar in Hebrew, so the discrepancy is likely due to an oral transmissional error.
tn Heb “and this temple which I consecrated for my name I will throw away from before my face.”
n1400735: tn Heb “him,” which appears in context to refer to Israel (i.e., “you” in direct address). Many translations understand the direct object of the verb “make” to be the temple (NEB, NASB, NIV, NRSV “it”).
n1400736: tn Heb “and I will make him [i.e., Israel] a proverb and a taunt,” that is, a proverbial example of destruction and an object of reproach.
n1400737: tn Heb “and this house which was high/elevated.” The statement makes little sense in this context, which predicts the desolation that judgment will bring. n1400814: tn The Hebrew phrase ‏ הַסֻּכּוֹת‎[‏חַג‎] (khag hassukkot, “[Feast of] shelters” [or “huts”]) is traditionally known as the Feast of Tabernacles. The rendering “booths” (cf. n1400821: tn The Hebrew word ‏כִּכַּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or, by extension, to a standard unit of weight. According to the older (Babylonian) standard the “talent” weighed 130 lbs. (58.9 kg), but later this was lowered to 108.3 lbs. (49.1 kg). More recent research suggests the “light” standard talent was 67.3 lbs. (30.6 kg). Using this as the standard for calculation, the weight of the gold was 30,285 lbs. (13,770 kg).

n1401119: sn Concubines were slave women in ancient Near Eastern societies who were the legal property of their master, but who could have legitimate sexual relations with their master. A concubine’s status was more elevated than a mere servant, but she was not free and did not have the legal rights of a free wife. The children of a concubine could, in some instances, become equal heirs with the children of the free wife. After the period of the Judges concubines may have become more of a royal prerogative (2 Sam 21:10-14; 1 Kgs 11:3).

n1401819: sn One does not expect Micaiah, having just vowed to speak only what the Lord tells him, to agree with the other prophets and give the king an inaccurate prophecy. Micaiah’s actions became understandable later, when we discover that the Lord desires to deceive the king and lead him to his demise. The Lord even dispatches a lying spirit to deceive Ahab’s prophets. Micaiah can lie to the king because he realizes this lie is from the Lord. It is important to note that in v. 13 Micaiah only vows to speak the word of his God; he does not necessarily say he will tell the truth. In this case the Lord’s word is deliberately deceptive. Only when the king adjures him to tell the truth (v. 15), does Micaiah do so.

n1401823: tn Heb “the spirit.” The significance of the article prefixed to ‏רוּחַ‎ (ruakh) is uncertain, but it could contain a clue as to this spirit’s identity, especially when interpreted in light of verse 23. It is certainly possible, and probably even likely, that the article is used in a generic or dramatic sense and should be translated, “a spirit.” In the latter case it would show that this spirit was vivid and definite in the mind of Micaiah the storyteller. However, if one insists that the article indicates a well-known or universally known spirit, the following context provides a likely referent. Verse 23 tells how Zedekiah slapped Micaiah in the face and then asked sarcastically, “Which way did the spirit from the Lord (‏רוּחַ־יְהוָה‎, ruakh yehvah) go when he went from me to speak to you?” When the phrase “the spirit of the Lord” refers to the divine spirit (rather than the divine breath or mind, as in Isa 40:7, 13) elsewhere, the spirit energizes an individual or group for special tasks or moves one to prophesy. This raises the possibility that the deceiving spirit of vv. 20-22 is the same as the divine spirit mentioned by Zedekiah in v. 23. This would explain why the article is used on ‏רוּחַ‎ (ruakh); he can be called “the spirit” because he is the well-known spirit who energizes the prophets.
n1401824: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1401825: tn The Hebrew text has two imperfects connected by ‏וְגַם‎ (vegam). These verbs could be translated as specific futures, “you will deceive and also you will prevail,” in which case the Lord is assuring the spirit of success on his mission. However, in a commissioning context (note the following imperatives) such as this, it is more likely that the imperfects are injunctive, in which case one could translate, “Deceive, and also overpower.”

n1401828: tn The Hebrew verbal forms could be imperatives (“Disguise yourself and enter”), but this would make no sense in light of the immediately following context. The forms are better interpreted as infinitives absolute functioning as cohortatives (see IBHS 594 §35.5.2a). Some prefer to emend the forms to imperfects.

Yet v. 10 goes on to state that the Edomite revolt was successful. The translation above assumes an emendation of the Hebrew text. Adding a third masculine singular pronominal suffix to the accusative sign before Edom (reading ‏אֹתוֹ‎ [ʾoto, “him”] instead of just ‏אֶת‎ [ʾet]) and taking Edom as the subject of verbs allows one to translate the verse in a way that is more consistent with the context, which depicts an Israelite defeat, not victory. n1402513: tn The Hebrew word ‏כִּכַּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or, by extension, to a standard unit of weight. According to the older (Babylonian) standard the “talent” weighed 130 lbs. (58.9 kg), but later this was lowered to 108.3 lbs. (49.1 kg). More recent research suggests the “light” standard talent was 67.3 lbs. (30.6 kg). Using this as the standard for calculation, the weight of the silver was 6,730 lbs. (3,060 kg).

n1402827: sn The Negev is an area of central, southern Judah, south of the hill country and Beer Sheba and west of the rift valley.
n1402829: sn That is, “of Judah.” Frequently in 2 Chronicles “Israel” is substituted for “Judah.”

n1402832: tn Heb “and he caused him distress and did not strengthen him.”
n1402833: tn Heb “divided up,” but some read ‏חִלֵּץ‎ (khillets, “despoiled”).
n1402834: tn Heb “the gods of Damascus, the ones who had defeated him.” The words “he thought” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The perspective is that of Ahaz, not the narrator! Another option is that “the kings” has been accidentally omitted after “gods of.” See v. 23b.
n1402836: tn Heb “As for the rest of his events, and all his ways, the former and the latter, look, they are written on the scroll of the kings of Judah and Israel.”
n140293: tn The parallel passage in 2 Kgs 18:2 has “Abi.”
n140294: tn Heb “he did what was proper in the eyes of the Lord, according to all which David his father had done.”
n140299: tn Heb “and he made them [an object] of dread and devastation and hissing.”
n1402913: tn Heb “so that the rage of his anger might turn from us.” The jussive with vav (‏ו‎) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding statement of intention.
n1402914: tn That is, to conduct the religious rituals directed to the Lord.
n1402915: tn That is, to be his ministers for the nation.
n1402916: tn Heb “ones who cause [sacrifices] to go up in smoke.” The Hiphil form of ‏קָטַר‎ (qatar) can refer specifically to offering incense (e.g. 2 Chr 26:19; 32:12), but it may also be a general word for making sacrifices (e.g. 1 Chr 6:49). If it refers to burning incense, then the altar of incense in the Holy place of the tabernacle may be in view. Otherwise it is more general (they sacrifice animals later in this chapter, 2 Chr 29:21-24) and includes making sacrifices as well as offering incense.
n1402921: sn Perhaps these terms refer metonymically to the royal court, the priests and Levites, and the people, respectively.
n1402922: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the king) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1402924: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Hezekiah) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

n1402932: tn Heb “and the number of burnt sacrifices which the assembly brought was seventy bulls, one hundred rams, two hundred lambs; for a burnt sacrifice to the Lord were all these.”
n1402933: tn The Hebrew term ‏צֹאן‎ (tsoʾn) denotes smaller livestock in general; depending on context it can refer to sheep only or goats only, but there is nothing in the immediate context here to specify one or the other.
n1402935: tn Heb “for the Levites were more pure of heart to consecrate themselves than the priests.”
n1402938: tn Heb “the people.” The pronoun “them” has been used here for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.
n140303: tn Heb “and the thing was proper in the eyes of.”
n140304: tn Heb “and they caused to stand a word to cause a voice to pass through.”

n140308: tn Heb “and according to the command of the king, saying.”
n140309: tn The jussive with vav conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.
n1403010: tn Heb “to the survivors who are left to you from the palm of the kings of Assyria.”
n1403012: tn Heb “and he made them a devastation” (or, perhaps, “an object of horror”).
n1403013: tn Heb “don’t stiffen your neck” (a Hebrew idiom for being stubborn).
n1403014: tn Heb “give a hand.” On the meaning of the idiom here, see HALOT 387 s.v. I ‏יָד‎ 2.
n1403015: tn Heb “so that the rage of his anger might turn from you.” The jussive with vav conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.
n1403017: tn Heb “and they were mocking them and ridiculing them.”

n1403020: tn Heb “and they arose and removed the altars which were in Jerusalem, and all the incense altars they removed and threw into the Kidron Valley.”

n1403026: tn Heb “everyone [who] has prepared his heart to seek God.”

n1403030: tn Heb “and they were praising the Lord day by day, the Levites and the priests with instruments of strength to the Lord.” The phrase ‏בִּכְלֵי־עֹז‎ (bikhle ʿoz, “with instruments of strength”) might refer to loud sounding musical instruments (NASB “with loud instruments”; NEB “with unrestrained fervour”). The present translation assumes an emendation to ‏בְּכָל־עֹז‎ (bekhol ʿoz, “with all strength”); see 1 Chr 13:8, as well as HALOT 805 s.v. I ‏עֹז‎ and BDB 739 s.v. ‏עֹז‎).
n1403031: tn Heb “and Hezekiah spoke to the heart of all the Levites.” On the meaning of the idiom “speak to the heart of” here, see HALOT 210 s.v. II ‏דבר‎ 8.d.
n1403032: tn Heb “who demonstrated skill [with] good skill for the Lord.”
n1403033: tn Heb “and they ate [during] the appointed time [for] seven days.” ‏מוֹעֵד‎ (moʿed, “appointed time”) is probably an adverbial accusative of time referring to the festival. However, some understand it as metonymically referring to the food eaten during the festival. See BDB 417 s.v.
n1403034: tn The Hebrew term ‏צֹאן‎ (tsoʾn, translated “sheep” twice in this verse) denotes smaller livestock in general; depending on context it can refer to sheep only or goats only, but there is nothing in the immediate context here to specify one or the other.
n1403035: tn Heb “the assembly.” The pronoun “them” has been used in the translation for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy.
n1403037: sn The term ‏גֵּר‎ (ger) refers to a foreign resident, but with different social implications in different settings. In Mosaic Law the resident foreigner was essentially a naturalized citizen and convert to worshiping the God of Israel (see Exod 12:19, 48; Deut 29:10-13). Here the term refers to those who had immigrated (or fled as refugees) from the conquered northern kingdom as well as those already residents of the southern kingdom of Judah.
n1403038: tn Heb “and there was great joy in Jerusalem, for from the days of Solomon son of David, king of Israel, there was nothing like this in Jerusalem.”
n1403039: tn Heb “and it was heard with their voice.” BDB 1034 s.v. ‏שָׁמַע‎ Niph.4 interprets this to mean “hearing was granted to their voice.” It is possible that the name ‏יְהוָה‎ (yehvah, “the Lord”) has been accidentally omitted.
n140313: tn Heb “the high places and the altars from all Judah and Benjamin and in Ephraim and in Manasseh until finished.”
n140314: tn Heb “and the sons of Israel returned, each to his possession to their cities.”
n140315: tn Heb “and Hezekiah appointed the divisions of the priests and the Levites according to their divisions, each in accordance with his service for the priests and for the Levites.”

n1403117: tn Heb “they blessed the Lord and his people Israel.”
n1403122: tn Heb “like great, like small” (i.e., old and young alike).
n1403123: tn Heb “in addition enrolling them by males from a son of three years and upwards, to everyone who enters the house of the Lord for a matter of a day in its day, for their service by their duties according to their divisions.”

n1403126: tn Heb “and in all the work which he began with regard to the service of the house of God and with respect to the law and with respect to the commandment, to seek his God; with all his heart he acted and he succeeded.”

n140324: tn Heb “and they closed up all the springs and the stream that flows in the midst of the land.” Here ‏אָרֶץ‎ (ʾarets, “land”) does not refer to the entire land, but to a smaller region like a district.
n140326: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Hezekiah) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

n1403210: tn Heb “and he placed officers of war over the people.”
n1403213: tn Heb “for with us [is] a greater [one] than with him.”

n1403217: tn Heb “all Judah.” The words “the people of” are supplied in the translation for clarity. The Hebrew text uses the name “Judah” here by metonymy for the people of Judah.
n1403218: tn Heb “On what are you trusting that [you] are living during the siege in Jerusalem.”
n1403220: tn Heb “Is not Hezekiah misleading you to give you over to die by hunger and thirst, saying, ‘The Lord our God will rescue us from the hand of the king of Assyria’?’
n1403221: tn Heb “Did not he, Hezekiah, eliminate…?” This rhetorical question presupposes a positive reply (“yes, he did”) and so has been translated here as a positive statement.
n1403222: tn Heb “his”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1403223: tn Heb “fathers” (also in vv. 14, 15), but in this context the term does not necessarily refer to Sennacherib’s ancestors, but to his predecessors on the Assyrian throne.
n1403227: tn The verb is plural, suggesting that the preceding ‏אֱלֹהֵיכֶם‎ (ʾelohekhem) be translated “your gods,” rather than “your God.”
n1403228: tn Heb “his”; the referent (Sennacherib) has been specified in the translation for clarity and for stylistic reasons.
n1403231: tn Heb “Like the gods of the nations of the lands who did not rescue their people from my hand, so the god of Hezekiah will not rescue his people from my hand.”
n1403233: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Sennacherib) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1403234: tn Heb “and he returned with shame of face to his land.”
n1403235: tn Heb “and some from those who went out from him, from his inward parts.”
n1403237: tc The Hebrew text reads literally, “and he led him from all around.” However, the present translation prefers the Septuagint and Vulgate reading, which suggests an original text of ‏וַיָּנַח לָהֶם מִסָּבִיב‎ (vayyanakh lahem missaviv, “and he gave rest to them from all around”). n1403240: tn Heb “was sick to the point of dying.”

n1403243: tn Heb “and Hezekiah humbled himself in the height of his heart, he and the residents of Jerusalem, and the anger of the Lord did not come upon them in the days of Hezekiah.”
n1403244: tn Heb “and stalls for all beasts and beasts, and flocks for the stalls.” The repetition of ‏בְּהֵמָה‎ (behemah, “beast”) here indicates various kinds of livestock.
n1403246: sn The phrase the City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.
n1403247: tn Heb “and when the envoys of the officials of Babylon, who sent to him to inquire concerning the sign which was in the land, [arrived].”

n1403251: tn Heb “and honor they did to him in his death, all Judah and the residents of Jerusalem.”
n140333: tn Heb “like the abominable practices of the nations.”
n140334: tn The phrase ‏כָל צְבָא הֲַשָּׁמַיִם‎ (khol tsevaʾ hashamayim), traditionally translated “all the host of heaven,” refers to the heavenly lights, including stars and planets. In 1 Kgs 22:19 these heavenly bodies are pictured as members of the Lord’s royal court or assembly, but many other texts view them as the illegitimate objects of pagan and Israelite worship.
n140336: tn Heb “In Jerusalem my name will be permanently.”
n140337: tn Or “he sacrificed his sons in the fire.” This may refer to child sacrifice, though some interpret it as a less drastic cultic practice (NEB, NASB “made his sons pass through the fire”; NIV “sacrificed his sons in the fire”; NRSV “made his sons pass through fire”). For discussion see M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 266-67.
n140338: tn Heb “and he set up a ritual pit, along with a conjurer.” Hebrew ‏אוֹב‎ (ʾov, “ritual pit”) refers to a pit used by a magician to conjure up underworld spirits. In 1 Sam 28:7 the witch of Endor is called a ‏בַּעֲלַת אוֹב‎ (baʿalat ʾov, “owner of a ritual pit”). See H. Hoffner, “Second Millennium Antecedents to the Hebrew ʾÔḆ,” JBL 86 (1967): 385-401.
n140339: tn Heb “and he multiplied doing what is evil in the eyes of the Lord, angering him.”
n1403310: tn Heb “In this house and in Jerusalem, which I chose from all the tribes of Israel, I will place my name permanently” (or perhaps “forever”).
n1403311: tn Heb “I will not again make the feet of Israel wander from the land which I established for their fathers.”
n1403312: tn Heb “misled Judah.” The words “the people of” are supplied in the translation for clarity. The Hebrew text uses the name “Judah” here by metonymy for the people of Judah.
n1403316: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Manasseh) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1403317: tn Heb “appeased the face of the Lord his God.”
n1403320: tn Heb “him”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

n1403324: tn Heb “he”; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

n1403326: sn The phrase the City of David refers here to the fortress of Zion in Jerusalem, not to Bethlehem. See 2 Sam 5:7.
n1403327: tn Heb “told Judah.” The words “the people of” are supplied in the translation for clarity. The Hebrew text uses the name “Judah” here by metonymy for the people of Judah.
n1403330: tn Heb “and his prayer and being entreated by him, and all his sin and his unfaithfulness and the places where he built high places and set up Asherah poles and idols before he humbled himself—behold, they are written on the words of his seers.”
n1403335: tn Heb “as Manasseh his father had humbled himself.”
n140342: tn Heb “he did what was proper in the eyes of the Lord.”
n140343: tn Heb “and walked in the ways of David his father.”
n140346: tn Heb “and they tore down before him the altars of the Baals.”
n140347: tn Heb “the priests”; the qualifying adjective “pagan” has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n140348: tn “In their ruins” is the marginal reading (Qere) of the Hebrew text.
n140349: tn Heb “to purify the land and the house.”
n1403410: tn Heb “from Manasseh and Ephraim.” The words “the people of” are supplied in the translation for clarity. The Hebrew text uses the names “Manasseh and Ephraim” here by metonymy for the people of Manasseh and Ephraim.
n1403411: tn Heb “all Judah and Benjamin.” The words “the people of” are supplied in the translation for clarity. The Hebrew text uses the names “Judah and Benjamin” here by metonymy for the people of Judah and Benjamin.
n1403412: tc The Hebrew consonantal text (Kethib) assumes the reading, “and the residents of.” The marginal reading (Qere) is “and they returned.”
n1403414: tn Heb “and they gave it to the doers of the work who were working in the house of the Lord to restore and to repair the house.”
n1403415: tn Heb “of the houses that the kings of Judah had destroyed.”
n1403416: tn Heb “[were] over the laborers and were directing every doer of work for work assignment and work assignment.”
n1403417: tn Heb “returned still the king a word, saying.”
n1403418: tn Heb “that was found in the house of the Lord.”
n1403419: tn The parallel account in 2 Kgs 22:12 has the variant spelling “Achbor son of Micaiah.”
n1403420: sn The implication is to seek a prophetic oracle.
n1403421: tc The MT reads ‏נִתְּכָה‎ (nittekah, “has gushed forth”) but the LXX implies ‏נִצְתָה‎ (nitsetah, “has been ignited”).
n1403423: tn Heb “and those who [were sent by] the king.”
n1403424: tn The parallel account in 2 Kgs 22:14 has the variant spelling “son of Tikvah, the son of Harhas.”
n1403426: tn Or “second.” For a discussion of the possible location of this district, see M. Cogan and H. Tadmor, II Kings (AB), 283.
n1403427: tn Heb “and they spoke to her like this.”

However, it is possible that this is a general reference to their sinful practices, in which case one might translate, “angering me by all the things they do.”
n1403431: tn Heb “Therefore, behold, I am gathering you to your fathers, and you will be gathered to your tomb in peace.”
n1403432: tn Heb “and the king sent and gathered all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.”
n1403433: tc This assumes an emendation to ‏עַמּוּדוֹ‎ (ʿammudo), see 23:13. The MT reads “at his place.”

n140357: tn Heb “and they put aside the burnt offering[s] to give them to the divisions of the house of the fathers for the sons of the people to bring near to the Lord as it is written in the scroll of Moses—and the same with the cattle.”
n140359: tn Heb “After all this, [by] which Josiah prepared the temple.”
n1403510: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n1403511: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Neco) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1403512: tn Heb “What to me and to you, king of Judah?”
n1403513: tn Heb “Not against you, you, today, but against the house of my battle.”
n1403514: tn Heb “Stop yourself from [opposing] God who is with me and let him not destroy you.”
n1403515: tn Heb “and Josiah did not turn his face from him.”
n1403521: tn Heb “and his faithful acts according to what is written in the law of the Lord.”
n140363: tn The Hebrew word ‏כִּכַּר‎ (kikkar, “circle”) refers generally to something that is round. When used of metals it can refer to a disk-shaped weight made of the metal or, by extension, to a standard unit of weight. According to the older (Babylonian) standard the “talent” weighed 130 lbs. (58.9 kg), but later this was lowered to 108.3 lbs. (49.1 kg). More recent research suggests the “light” standard talent was 67.3 lbs. (30.6 kg). Using this as the standard for calculation, the weight of the silver was 6,730 lbs. (3,060 kg).
n140364: tn Heb “his”; the referent (Jehoahaz) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1403610: tn Heb “in Babylon.” Repeating the proper name “Babylon” here would be redundant in contemporary English, so “there” has been used in the translation.
n1403611: tn Heb “As for the rest of the events of Jehoiakim, and his horrible deeds which he did and that which was found against him, look, they are written on the scroll of the kings of Israel and Judah.”
n1403612: tc The Hebrew text reads “eight,” but some ancient textual witnesses, as well as the parallel text in 2 Kgs 24:8, have “eighteen.”
n1403616: tn Heb “and he made Zedekiah his brother king.” According to the parallel text in 2 Kgs 24:17, Zedekiah was Jehoiachin’s uncle, not his brother. Therefore many interpreters understand ‏אח‎ (ʾakh) here in its less specific sense of “relative” (NEB “made his father’s brother Zedekiah king”; NASB “made his kinsman Zedekiah king”; NIV “made Jehoiachin’s uncle, Zedekiah, king”; NRSV “made his brother Zedekiah king”).
n1403620: tn Heb “and he stiffened his neck and strengthened his heart from returning.”
n1403621: tn Heb “like all the abominable practices of the nations.”
n1403623: tn Heb “and the Lord God of their fathers sent against them by the hand of his messengers, getting up early and sending.”
n1403625: tn All three verbal forms (“mocked,” “despised,” and “ridiculed”) are active participles in the Hebrew text, indicating continual or repeated action. They made a habit of rejecting God’s prophetic messengers.
n1403626: tn Heb “until the anger of the Lord went up against his people until there was no healer.”
n1403630: tn Heb “he”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n1403633: tn The verb may be seen as either of two homophonous roots ‏רָצָה‎ (ratsah) meaning “to restore” or “to accept, take pleasure in.”
n1403634: sn According to Lev 25:4, the land was to remain uncultivated every seventh year. Lev 26:33-35 warns that the land would experience a succession of such sabbatical rests if the people disobeyed God, for he would send them away into exile.
n1403636: sn Concerning the seventy years see Jer 25:11. Cyrus’ edict (see vv. 22-23) occurred about fifty years after the fall of Jerusalem in 586 b.c., which is most naturally understood as the beginning point of the “days of desolation” mentioned in v. 21. The number “seventy” is probably used in a metaphorical sense, indicating a typical lifetime and suggesting a thorough or complete judgment that would not be lifted until an entirely new generation emerged.
n1403637: sn The first year of Cyrus would be ca. 539 b.c. Cyrus reigned in Persia from ca. 539-530 b.c.
n1403638:
tn Heb “by the mouth of Jeremiah.”
sn See Jer 25:11; 29:10.
n1403639: tn Heb “stirred the spirit of.” The Hebrew noun ‏רוּחַ‎ (ruakh, “spirit”) has a broad range of meanings (see BDB 924-26 s.v.). Here, it probably refers to (1) “mind” as the seat of mental acts (e.g., Exod 28:3; Deut 34:9; Isa 29:24; 40:13; Ezek 11:5; 20:32; 1 Chr 28:12; cf. BDB 925 s.v. 6) or (2) “will” as the seat of volitional decisions (e.g., Exod 35:5, 22; Pss 51:12, 14; 57:8; 2 Chr 29:31; cf. BDB 925 s.v. 7). So also in Ezra 1:5. The entire phrase “stirred the spirit” has been rendered as “motivated” to better reflect normal English.
n1403640: tn Heb “a voice.” The Hebrew noun ‏קוֹל‎ (qol, “voice, sound”) has a broad range of meanings, including the metonymical (cause—effect) nuance “proclamation” (e.g., Exod 36:6; 2 Chr 24:9; 30:5; 36:22; Ezra 1:1; 10:7; Neh 8:15). See BDB 877 s.v. 3.a.2.
